title: ResNet-18 vs 34 vs 50 vs 101
concept: The numbers in names like ResNet-18, ResNet-34, ResNet-50, and ResNet-101 mainly tell you layer count, but they also hint at a family split: 18 and 34 use basic blocks, while 50 and 101 use bottlenecks so added depth stays manageable.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which pair is most associated with the simpler basic-block side of the classic ResNet family?
exercises.explain: Right — 18 and 34 are the shallower basic-block siblings.

cards.title: The numbers are labels, but not random labels
cards.body: When people say 'ResNet-50' or 'ResNet-101,' the numbers can sound like product codes. They are more useful than that. They tell you how deep the model is meant to be — and, in practice, they also signal which block style the model likely uses.

cards.title: What the number mostly means
cards.body: In the classic naming scheme, the number in a ResNet name refers mainly to the model's layer count. So ResNet-34 is deeper than ResNet-18, and ResNet-101 is deeper than ResNet-50. But do not stop there: deeper names usually come with a bottleneck-style block design, not just more copies of the shallow block.

cards.title: Same building family, different floor plans
cards.body: Imagine a builder offering 18-floor, 34-floor, 50-floor, and 101-floor towers. The taller towers do not just repeat the exact same apartment layout forever. At some point the designer changes the internal floor plan so the huge building remains practical. That is what happens across the ResNet family.

cards.title: Reading the classic lineup
cards.body: A useful first-pass map is: ResNet-18 and ResNet-34 are simpler basic-block models; ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 are deeper bottleneck models. So when you see the number jump from 34 to 50, you should also expect a block-design shift, not just more repeated layers.

cards.title: Higher number does not guarantee 'better at everything'
cards.body: Bigger numbers usually mean more depth and often more capacity, but they also mean more compute and latency. The family names are not a universal leaderboard. They are depth-and-design choices made under different efficiency budgets.

cards.title: A simple family map
cards.body: Think of the names as two related subfamilies, not four isolated model numbers.

left.text:
Shallower classic members
Basic blocks
Simpler internal structure

right.text:
Deeper classic members
Bottleneck blocks
Depth scaled more efficiently

exercises.prompt: What is the best quick interpretation of the name ResNet-101?

options.text: It is a much deeper member of the ResNet family, typically using bottleneck blocks
It is the smallest ResNet and uses no residual shortcuts
It means every convolution has size 101x101
It is just a random marketing number with no architectural meaning

exercises.explain: Exactly — the number signals depth, and in this family it also points you toward the deeper bottleneck side.

exercises.prompt: True or false: once the classic family gets to ResNet-50 and beyond, the block style usually changes rather than merely repeating the ResNet-18 pattern forever.
exercises.explain: True — that switch to bottlenecks is a big part of how the deeper variants stay practical.

exercises.sentence: In the classic family, ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 are usually associated with ___ blocks.
exercises.explain: Right — the deeper classic variants are the bottleneck-style branch.

exercises.prompt: Which explanation makes the family relationship intuitive instead of sounding like trivia?

options.text: The numbers mainly track depth, and the family naturally splits into a simpler basic-block branch and a deeper bottleneck branch
The numbers only reflect how many images were used in training
The numbers matter only because higher numbers always win regardless of cost

exercises.explain: Yes — that framing helps you understand the lineup as one evolving design, not a memorized list.

exercises.prompt: A teammate says, 'ResNet-34 and ResNet-50 are almost the same idea, so 50 just means sixteen extra layers.' What is the best correction?

options.text: They share the residual idea, but ResNet-50 also shifts into bottleneck blocks so extra depth does not behave like a naive extension of the 34-layer design
ResNet-50 is actually a shallower model that only sounds bigger
The numbers compare image resolution, not network depth
ResNet-34 has no residual shortcuts, so the models are unrelated

exercises.explain: Exactly. The family names are easiest to understand when you track both the depth number and the block type. Next, we'll zoom out one level further and ask: what design knobs actually change across ResNet variants overall?
